title: Basic Anatomy & Physiology
description: Basic Anatomy & Physiology content organized by the course index. The first index currently contains the imported slide content; remaining index sections are ready for future PPT/doc data.

modules.title: 1. Basic to Anatomy & Physiology
modules.summary: Foundational terminology and introductory anatomy/physiology slides.

lessons.title: Medical terminology is the language of medicine

blocks.text: It may seem complex at first, but once the structure is understood, the terms become easy to interpret. Medical words are built from prefixes, suffixes, and root words.

items.text: Suffix - Ending; indicates condition or procedure.
Meaning of the full term = meaning of each part combined.

children.text: Meaning: abnormal condition of fluid accumulation in the kidney.

blocks.text: A prefix modifies the meaning of a word. It usually consists of one or two syllables and is placed before the root word.
The ending that follows a root word is a suffix. The suffix usually consists of one or more syllables.
Root word = foundation of a medical term. It indicates the main body organ or anatomical part.

Medical terms are built by combining a root word with a prefix and/or suffix to create the appropriate meaning. In the examples, the root word nephr, meaning the functional unit of the kidney, is used to construct several different terms.
When two consonants fall together, a combining vowel is introduced to make pronunciation easier.

rows: Inflammation of renal pelvis and kidney
Abnormal condition of fluid in the kidney
Inflammation affecting many areas of the kidney

blocks.text: Anatomy: study of the structure of the body.
Physiology: study of how the body functions.

blocks.title: Levels of Organization of the Human Body

items.text: Tissue Level - groups of similar cells.
Organ Level - two or more tissues working together.
System Level - group of organs working together.
No body system works in isolation.
Goal: homeostasis - maintenance of stable internal environment.
Respiratory system brings in oxygen and removes carbon dioxide.
Circulatory system delivers oxygen to cells and carries carbon dioxide away.
Both systems work together to maintain life.

blocks.text: Body cavities are spaces within the body that protect internal organs, support organs, and separate organs.

items.text: The body cavities are divided into two main groups
Dorsal Body Cavity: located at the back of the body

children.text: Spinal (vertebral) cavity: contains the spinal cord.

items.text: Ventral Body Cavity: located at the front of the body and divided by the diaphragm

children.text: Thoracic cavity: located above the diaphragm; contains two pleural cavities, mediastinum, and pericardial cavity.
Abdominopelvic cavity: located below the diaphragm.
Abdominal cavity: contains stomach, liver, spleen, pancreas, and intestines.
Pelvic cavity: contains urinary bladder, reproductive organs, and rectum.

blocks.text: The abdomen is divided into four quadrants by a vertical line and a horizontal line crossing at the umbilicus (navel).

items.text: Prone: lying flat on the abdomen with the face downward.
Supine: lying flat on the back with the face upward.
Right Lateral Recumbent: lying on the right side of the body.
Left Lateral Recumbent: lying on the left side of the body.
Fowler's Position: semi-sitting position with the head and upper body elevated.
Trendelenburg Position: supine position with the head lower than the feet.

modules.title: 3. Blood and Circulatory System
modules.summary: Overview of the circulatory system, blood vessels, heart anatomy, blood components, glossary terms, and common circulatory conditions.

children.text: Carry oxygen, nutrients, and chemicals in the blood to the body's cells.
Remove waste products from the cells and carry them for elimination.
Pulmonary circulation: right ventricle -> lungs -> left atrium; oxygen absorbed; carbon dioxide excreted.
Systemic circulation: left ventricle -> aorta -> body tissues; returns via venae cavae -> right atrium; supplies oxygen and nutrients to the body.
Portal circulation: digestive organs and spleen -> liver; blood passes through the liver before returning to the heart.
Carries oxygenated blood to the body.
Return deoxygenated blood to the right atrium.

items.text: The heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body.
Normal heart rate: 60-80 beats per minute.
The heartbeat is controlled by the sinoatrial (SA) node - the natural pacemaker.

children.text: Pump blood out of the heart.
Muscular wall separating right and left sides.
Prevents mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.

lessons.title: Heart Valves and Layers of the Heart Wall

children.text: Located between left ventricle and aorta.
Prevents backflow into the left ventricle.
Between left atrium and left ventricle.
Between right atrium and right ventricle.
Double membranous sac surrounding the heart.

items.text: Heart muscle receives oxygenated blood from
Blood is a connective tissue that circulates in blood vessels.

children.text: Plasma - liquid portion, mostly water plus dissolved substances.

items.text: Function: defend the body against infection.

children.text: Carry oxygen from lungs to body tissues.
Carry carbon dioxide from tissues back to lungs.

lessons.title: Glossary of Terms - Circulatory System I

rows: Largest artery in the body; arises out of the left ventricle of the heart and courses down the body trunk.
Curved portion of the aorta which courses upward then turns downward behind the heart to form an arch.
Distal end of the abdominal aorta at which point the artery divides into the two common iliac arteries.
Fibrous tissue flaps or leaflets which open and close between the left ventricle and ascending aorta. Valve closure prevents backflow of blood into the heart.
Elastic, extensible vessels which carry blood in the direction away from the heart.
One of the two upper chambers of the heart (right and left).
A microscopic vessel through which blood travels from arteriole to venule. Oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange occurs across capillary walls.
Vessels (common, external and internal) which provide the major blood supply to the head and neck.
Pertaining to movement around a circular route.
The resting stage of the cardiac cycle.
Hollow, muscular organ which pumps blood to all parts of the body.

lessons.title: Glossary of Terms - Circulatory System II

rows: Common vessel which carries major blood supply to each leg.
Also known as the subclavian artery.
The two-leaflet heart valve between the left atrium and ventricle of the heart. Closure prevents reflux of blood into the left atrium. The mitral valve is also called the bicuspid valve.
Fluid portion of blood in which cells are suspended.
Instead of returning directly to the heart, venous blood passes from the capillary bed of the abdominal part of the digestive system and the spleen via the capillary bed of the liver.
Vessel which carries venous blood from right ventricle of the heart to lung for oxygenation.
The circulation of blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs and back to the left atrium.
Vessel which returns oxygenated blood from the lung to the left atrium of the heart.
Erythrocytes carry oxygen and carbon dioxide between the lungs and all body cells.

Large vessel which branches off the aortic arch on the left and the brachiocephalic trunk on the right to supply blood to areas of the trunk, head and upper extremities. Also known as the innominate artery.

lessons.title: Glossary of Terms - Circulatory System III

rows: The circulation of blood from the left ventricle of the heart via the aorta to all parts of the body and back to the right atrium via the venae cavae.
The contracting stage of the cardiac cycle.
Valve with three fibrous tissue leaflets which open and close between the right atrium and ventricle of the heart. Closure prevents reflux of blood into the right atrium.
Vessel which carries blood in the direction toward the heart.
Two large veins, inferior and superior, which return venous blood to the right atrium of the heart.
One of the two lower chambers of the heart (right and left).

Two main varieties of white blood cells are granular leucocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils and basophils) and non-granular leucocytes (lymphocytes and monocytes). Their main function is to fight infection.

lessons.title: Conditions of the Circulatory System I

rows: Persistent bluish discoloration of extremities due to vasospasm
Localized dilation of a blood vessel wall
Aortic valve regurgitation into left ventricle
Sudden brain dysfunction due to stroke
Limb pain due to arterial insufficiency

lessons.title: Conditions of the Circulatory System II

rows: Right heart failure from lung disease
Chronic high BP without known cause
Congenital defect with four cardiac abnormalities
Abnormal heart sound due to turbulent flow
Severe BP elevation with organ damage
Death of heart muscle due to ischaemia

lessons.title: Conditions of the Circulatory System III

rows: Vasospasm of digital arteries causing color change
Hardening of tissue due to fibrosis
Severe reduction in granulocytes increasing infection risk
CBC with differential, bone marrow biopsy

lessons.title: Conditions of the Circulatory System IV

rows: Decrease in hemoglobin concentration or RBC count leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity of blood
CBC (low Hb), peripheral smear, iron studies, vitamin B12 levels
Iron therapy, vitamin B12/folate supplementation, blood transfusion (severe cases), treat underlying cause
Reduction in all three blood cell lines (RBCs, WBCs, and platelets)
CBC (low Hb, low WBC, low platelets), peripheral smear, bone marrow biopsy
Treat underlying cause, blood transfusion, immunosuppressive therapy, bone marrow transplant (severe cases)
